chunk_summary: This section outlines antimicrobial regimens for methicillin-susceptible and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus in bone infections, emphasizing drug choices, dosages, and treatment durations for pediatric patients.
learning_objectives: Understand the preferred antimicrobial regimens for methicillin-susceptible and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus in bone infections.
Learn appropriate pediatric dosing for cloxacillin, cefazolin, vancomycin, and other agents.
Recognize the importance of narrowing therapy to beta-lactams for methicillin-susceptible infections.
Identify adjunctive therapies and monitoring requirements for methicillin-resistant infections.
key_facts: Cloxacillin (200 mg/kg/day) and cefazolin (100 mg/kg/day) are preferred for methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus.
Vancomycin (45-60 mg/kg/day) is the standard for methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, with therapeutic drug monitoring required.
Oral step-down therapy for methicillin-susceptible infections includes high-dose cephalexin or cloxacillin.
Methicillin-resistant osteomyelitis typically requires 4-6 weeks of therapy.
Rifampicin should not be used alone due to rapid resistance development but is useful as an adjunct in biofilm-associated infections.
reference_quote: Cloxacillin remains the preferred targeted agent, with cefazolin as an effective alternative when dosing convenience or intolerance favors it. (Source: Indian Academy of Pediatrics Consensus Statement)
document_key_topics: Pediatric dosing guidelines for bone infections
